questions.question:
Let R(a, b, c) and S(d, e, f) be two relations in which b is the foreign key of R that refers to the primary key d of S. Consider the following four operations:
I. Insert into R
II. Insert into S
III. Delete from R
IV. Delete from S
Which of the following can cause violation of the referential integrity constraint above?
A. Both I and IV
B. Both II and III
C. All of these
D. None of these
questions.to_find: Which operations among insert/delete on R and S can violate the referential integrity constraint

understand.plain: We have two tables R and S where column b in R is a foreign key pointing to column d in S. Referential integrity means every value of b in R must have a matching value in d in S. Inserting a row into R with a b-value that does not exist in S violates this. Deleting a row from S whose d-value is still referenced by some row in R also violates this.

keywords.explain: A column (or set of columns) in one table that refers to the primary key of another table, enforcing a link between them.
keywords.example: b in R references d in S means every value of b must appear as d in S.

keywords.explain: The constraint that every foreign-key value in the referencing relation must match a primary-key value in the referenced relation.
keywords.example: If R has a row (1, 5, 'x') then S must have a row with d=5.

understand.visual_alt: R has columns a, b, c where b is a foreign key pointing to column d in S which has columns d, e, f.

given.aim: Identify which operations on R and S can violate the referential integrity constraint b -> d.

terms.meaning: Relation R with primary attributes a,b,c where b is the foreign key.
terms.example: R could hold student enrollment records.
terms.connects: Insertions into R can violate referential integrity if b has no match in S.

terms.meaning: Relation S with primary key d, referenced by R.b.
terms.connects: Deletions from S can violate referential integrity if d is still referenced by R.b.

terms.meaning: Every value of b in R must exist as a value of d in S.
terms.example: If b=10 exists in R, then d=10 must exist in S.
terms.connects: Any operation that breaks this link causes a violation.

given.plan: For each of the four operations, check whether it can introduce a b-value in R with no matching d in S (forward direction) or remove a d-value from S while some R still references it (reverse direction).

steps.title: Analyze Insert into R (Operation I)
steps.formula_raw: Violation if new R.b not in pi_d(S)
steps.apply: When we insert a new row into R, we are adding a new b-value. If that b-value does not exist in S.d, the referential integrity is violated. So Operation I CAN cause a violation.
steps.note: This is the most common way referential integrity is violated: inserting a child row without a matching parent.

steps.title: Analyze Insert into S (Operation II)
steps.apply: Inserting a row into S only adds more values to d. This can only satisfy existing constraints, never break them. Operation II CANNOT cause a violation.
steps.note: Adding a parent row never orphans a child.

steps.title: Analyze Delete from R (Operation III)
steps.apply: Deleting from R removes a row that contains a b-value. This reduces references to S, so it cannot cause a violation. Operation III CANNOT cause a violation.
steps.note: Removing a child row never breaks the parent-child link.

steps.title: Analyze Delete from S (Operation IV)
steps.formula_raw: Violation if deleted S.d still in pi_b(R)
steps.apply: When we delete a row from S, we remove a d-value. If some row in R still has that d-value as its b-value, those rows in R now have no matching parent in S, violating referential integrity. So Operation IV CAN cause a violation.
steps.note: This is the other classic violation: deleting a parent row that still has children.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Operations that can cause violation: I (Insert into R) and IV (Delete from S). This matches option A.
steps.note: Operations II and III can never violate referential integrity.

questions.formula_note: Referential integrity: for a foreign key b in R referencing primary key d in S, a violation occurs when inserting into the child table R with a missing parent, or deleting from the parent table S while children still exist in R.

questions.question:
The following table has two attributes A and C where A is the primary key and C is the foreign key referencing A with on-delete cascade.

A | C
------
2 | 4
3 | 4
4 | 3
5 | 2
7 | 2
9 | 5
6 | 4

The set of all tuples that must be additionally deleted to preserve referential integrity when the tuple (2, 4) is deleted is:
A. (3,4) and (6,4)
B. (5,2) and (7,2) and (9,5)
C. (5,2) and (7,2)
D. (3,4), (4,3), (6,4), (5,2), (7,2), and (9,5)
questions.to_find: Set of all tuples additionally deleted due to on-delete cascade when tuple (2,4) is deleted

understand.plain: We have a table with primary key A and foreign key C that references A. The on-delete cascade rule means: when a row with primary key value k is deleted, all rows whose C-value equals k are also automatically deleted. This cascades: those deleted rows may in turn trigger further cascading deletions. We need to find all rows deleted after initially deleting (2,4).

keywords.explain: A referential action that automatically deletes all child rows (rows whose foreign key matches the deleted parent's primary key) when a parent row is deleted.
keywords.example: Deleting row with A=2 cascades to delete all rows where C=2.

keywords.explain: A chain reaction of deletions triggered by on-delete cascade; deleted rows may themselves trigger further deletions.
keywords.example: If deleting A=2 removes rows with A=5 (because C=2), and A=5 is referenced by A=9 (C=5), then A=9 is also deleted.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 480 300" width="480" height="300"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="200" height="280" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="40" class="lbl ink">A  |  C</text><line x1="10" y1="48" x2="210" y2="48" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="12" y="50" width="196" height="28" class="no" opacity="0.3"/><text x="30" y="70" class="sub ink">2  |  4  (DELETED)</text><text x="30" y="100" class="sub ink">3  |  4</text><text x="30" y="130" class="sub ink">4  |  3</text><rect x="12" y="118" width="196" height="28" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="130" class="sub ink">4  |  3</text><rect x="12" y="148" width="196" height="28" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="160" class="sub ink">5  |  2  (cascade)</text><text x="30" y="190" class="sub ink">7  |  2  (cascade)</text><rect x="12" y="178" width="196" height="28" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="190" class="sub ink">7  |  2  (cascade)</text><rect x="12" y="208" width="196" height="28" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="220" class="sub ink">9  |  5  (cascade)</text><text x="30" y="250" class="sub ink">6  |  4</text><text x="240" y="80" class="sub mut">Delete (2,4)</text><text x="240" y="110" class="sub mut">C=2: remove (5,2),(7,2)</text><text x="240" y="140" class="sub mut">C=5: remove (9,5)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing tuple (2,4) being deleted, cascading to (5,2) and (7,2) because their C=2, then further cascading to (9,5) because its C=5 and A=5 was just deleted.

given.aim: Find all tuples deleted by on-delete cascade starting from deleting (2,4).

terms.meaning: The explicitly deleted row has A=2.
terms.example: A=2 is now gone from the table.
terms.connects: All rows with C=2 will now be cascade-deleted.

terms.term: C references A with on-delete cascade
terms.meaning: Whenever a row with primary key value v is deleted, every row whose C=v is also deleted.
terms.example: Delete A=5 -> also delete all rows where C=5.
terms.connects: Drives the chain of cascading deletions.

terms.example: Row (5,2) has C=2, so it points back to row with A=2.
terms.connects: We trace which rows have C equal to each deleted A value.

given.plan: Start with deletion of A=2. Find all rows with C=2 (cascade delete them). Then for each newly deleted row, find rows with C equal to that deleted A value. Repeat until no more cascading occurs.

steps.title: Delete initial tuple (2,4); A=2 is removed
steps.formula_raw: Cascade: for each deleted row with A=v, delete all rows where C=v
steps.apply: Deleting (2,4) means A=2 is gone. Find all rows where C=2: rows (5,2) and (7,2). These are cascade-deleted.
steps.note: Rows (3,4) and (6,4) have C=4, not C=2, so they are NOT deleted in this step.

steps.title: Cascade from deletion of (5,2): A=5 is removed
steps.apply: Row (5,2) is deleted (A=5 gone). Find all rows where C=5: row (9,5). Row (9,5) is cascade-deleted.

steps.title: Cascade from deletion of (7,2): A=7 is removed
steps.apply: Row (7,2) is deleted (A=7 gone). Find all rows where C=7: none. No further cascade.

steps.title: Cascade from deletion of (9,5): A=9 is removed
steps.apply: Row (9,5) is deleted (A=9 gone). Find all rows where C=9: none. No further cascade.

steps.title: Collect all additionally deleted tuples
steps.apply: Additionally deleted (beyond the original (2,4)): (5,2), (7,2), (9,5). This matches option B.
steps.note: Tuples (3,4) and (6,4) are NOT deleted because they reference A=4, which was not deleted.

questions.formula_note: On-delete cascade triggers a chain reaction: deleting a parent row automatically deletes all child rows referencing it, and this propagates recursively until no more children exist.

questions.question:
Suppose R1 and R2 are two relation schemas. Let r1 and r2 be the corresponding relation instances. B is a foreign key that refers to C in R2. If data in r1 and r2 satisfy referential integrity constraints, which of the following is ALWAYS TRUE?
A. pi_B(r1) subset_of pi_C(r2)
B. pi_C(r2) subset_of pi_B(r1)
C. pi_B(r1) = pi_C(r2)
D. pi_B(r1) intersect pi_C(r2) != empty
questions.to_find: Which set-theoretic relation between the projection of the foreign key column B in r1 and the primary key column C in r2 is always guaranteed by referential integrity

understand.plain: R1 has a foreign key B that references column C in R2. Referential integrity says every value that appears in B (in table r1) must also appear in C (in table r2). This means the set of B-values is a subset of the set of C-values. However, r2 may contain C-values that no row in r1 references, so the inclusion is one-directional.

keywords.explain: pi_B(r1) means the set of all distinct values of column B in relation r1.
Set A is a subset of set B if every element of A also appears in B.
keywords.example: {10,20} subset_of {10,20,30} is true; {10,40} subset_of {10,20,30} is false.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 220" width="400" height="220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx="260" cy="110" rx="110" ry="80" fill="#CECBF6" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.6"/><ellipse cx="230" cy="110" rx="60" ry="50" fill="#F1EFE8" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><text x="200" y="115" class="lbl ink">pi_B(r1)</text><text x="290" y="115" class="sub mut">pi_C(r2)</text><text x="30" y="50" class="sub ink">pi_B(r1) subset_of pi_C(r2)</text><text x="30" y="75" class="sub ink">Every B-value in r1 must exist</text><text x="30" y="95" class="sub ink">as a C-value in r2.</text><text x="30" y="120" class="sub ink">r2 may have extra C-values</text><text x="30" y="140" class="sub ink">not referenced by r1.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram where the set pi_B(r1) is entirely contained within the larger set pi_C(r2), illustrating that every foreign-key value in r1 must exist as a primary-key value in r2, but not vice-versa.

given.aim: Determine the always-true set relationship between pi_B(r1) and pi_C(r2) given referential integrity.

terms.meaning: The set of all distinct B-values appearing in relation instance r1.
terms.connects: These are the foreign key values that must all exist in r2.

terms.meaning: The set of all distinct C-values (primary key values) in relation instance r2.
terms.connects: This is the domain of legal foreign key values.

terms.meaning: For every tuple t1 in r1, t1[B] must equal t2[C] for some tuple t2 in r2.
terms.example: If r1 has B=5, then C=5 must exist somewhere in r2.

given.plan: Translate the referential integrity definition into set notation. The definition says every B-value in r1 has a matching C-value in r2. In set terms this is pi_B(r1) subset_of pi_C(r2). Check each option against this definition.

steps.title: State the referential integrity definition
steps.formula_raw: For all t in r1, there exists s in r2 such that t[B] = s[C]
steps.apply: This definition means: every value that appears as B in r1 must appear as C in r2. Equivalently, pi_B(r1) subset_of pi_C(r2).
steps.note: This is the precise formal definition of referential integrity.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: pi_B(r1) subset_of pi_C(r2)
steps.apply: This is exactly what referential integrity guarantees. If B references C and the constraint is satisfied, then every B-value exists in C. This is ALWAYS TRUE.
steps.note: This is the direct logical equivalent of the referential integrity definition.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: pi_C(r2) subset_of pi_B(r1)
steps.apply: This is NOT always true. r2 may have C-values that no row in r1 references. Example: r2 has C={5,10,15} but r1 only references B={5,10}. Then 15 is in pi_C(r2) but not in pi_B(r1).
steps.note: Referential integrity is a one-way constraint: child must reference valid parent, but parent need not be referenced.

steps.title: Evaluate options C and D
steps.apply: C (equality) requires both subsets, which is stronger than necessary - NOT always true. D (non-empty intersection) is implied by A if r1 is non-empty, but is not as precise as A and fails if r1 is empty.
steps.note: Option A is the most precise and always-true characterization.

questions.formula_note: Referential integrity in set notation: pi_{FK}(child_relation) subset_of pi_{PK}(parent_relation); the set of foreign key values in the child table is always a subset of primary key values in the parent table.

questions.question:
Consider the following tables T1 and T2:

T1:
P | Q
-----
2 | 2
3 | 8
7 | 3
5 | 8
6 | 9

T2:
R | S
-----
2 | 2
8 | 3
2 | 2
8 | 3
7 | 7

In table T2, R is the primary key and Q in table T1 is the foreign key referencing R in table T2 with on-delete cascade and on-update set NULL. In the table T1, P is the primary key. The number of additional records that need to be deleted from table T1 to preserve referential integrity, when the tuple (8, 3) is deleted from T2, is ____
questions.to_find: Number of additional records deleted from T1 due to referential integrity when (8,3) is deleted from T2

understand.plain: Table T1 has a foreign key Q that references the primary key R of T2, with on-delete cascade. When we delete a row from T2 with R=8, all rows in T1 whose Q=8 must also be deleted (cascading). We need to count how many rows in T1 have Q=8.

keywords.explain: When a referenced parent row is deleted, all child rows referencing it via foreign key are automatically deleted.
keywords.example: Delete T2 row with R=8 -> all T1 rows where Q=8 are deleted.

keywords.explain: R is the primary key of T2, so each R value is unique in T2.
keywords.example: Deleting (8,3) from T2 removes R=8 entirely from T2.

understand.visual_alt: T1 table has rows (2,2),(3,8),(7,3),(5,8),(6,9). T2 table has R as primary key. Deleting T2 row with R=8 means all T1 rows with Q=8 must be cascade-deleted: rows (3,8) and (5,8).

given.aim: Count T1 rows that must be deleted when T2 row (8,3) is deleted, due to on-delete cascade on foreign key Q referencing R.

terms.meaning: Rows in T1 whose foreign key Q matches the deleted primary key R=8 in T2.
terms.example: From T1: (3,8) has Q=8, (5,8) has Q=8.
terms.connects: These are the rows that must be cascade-deleted.

terms.meaning: R=8, S=3 is the row being explicitly deleted from T2.
terms.example: After deletion, R=8 no longer exists in T2.
terms.connects: Triggers cascade deletion of all T1 rows where Q=8.

terms.meaning: Referential action that deletes child rows automatically when the referenced parent is deleted.
terms.example: No manual action needed; the DBMS handles it.
terms.connects: We need to count how many rows in T1 satisfy Q=8.

given.plan: Find all rows in T1 where Q equals the deleted primary key value (R=8). Count those rows - that is the number of additional records deleted.

steps.title: Identify the deleted primary key value
steps.apply: The tuple deleted from T2 is (8,3). The primary key R=8 is no longer in T2 after this deletion.
steps.note: R is the primary key of T2, so deleting (8,3) means R=8 is completely removed.

steps.title: Find T1 rows with Q=8 (cascade-deleted rows)
steps.apply: Scanning T1: (2,2)->Q=2 no, (3,8)->Q=8 YES, (7,3)->Q=3 no, (5,8)->Q=8 YES, (6,9)->Q=9 no. Rows (3,8) and (5,8) must be cascade-deleted.
steps.note: Exactly 2 rows in T1 have Q=8.

steps.title: Check for further cascading
steps.apply: T1 has primary key P. After deleting (3,8) and (5,8), P=3 and P=5 are removed. Since Q in T1 references R in T2, not P in T1, there is no further cascading within T1. No further rows are affected.
steps.note: The foreign key Q in T1 references T2, not T1 itself, so no self-referential cascade.

steps.title: State the final count
steps.apply: The number of additional records deleted from T1 is 2 (the rows (3,8) and (5,8)).
steps.note: The on-update set NULL clause is irrelevant here since we are performing a delete, not an update.

questions.formula_note: On-delete cascade: count |sigma_{FK=v}(child_table)| where v is the primary key value of the deleted parent row to find how many additional child rows are removed.
